source: Software engineering management & technical coordination (professional grade)

questions.question: A team receives a request to build a REST API that will handle approximately 500 requests per second with complex JSON transformations. Which language choice best balances developer productivity and performance for this scenario?

choices.A: Python, because its dynamic typing makes JSON manipulation trivial
choices.B: Go, because its concurrency model and compiled performance handle high-throughput APIs well
choices.C: Java, because the JVM startup time is irrelevant for long-running servers
choices.D: Ruby, because its metaprogramming capabilities simplify JSON handling

questions.explanation: At 500 req/s with complex transformations, Go provides excellent throughput via goroutines and compiled performance, while remaining productive for API development. Python could work but may struggle at this throughput without significant async optimization.

questions.question: When assigning a task to build a real-time chat feature, which developer specialization is MOST critical to match?

choices.A: DevOps specialist, since deployment is the primary bottleneck
choices.B: Frontend CSS specialist, since chat UI design is the hardest part
choices.C: Database optimization specialist, since chat requires fast reads
choices.D: WebSocket and event-driven programming specialist, since real-time requires persistent bidirectional connections

questions.explanation: Real-time chat fundamentally depends on persistent bidirectional communication (WebSockets, SSE). A developer experienced with event-driven patterns and connection management is the most critical match for this task.

questions.question: A developer estimates a task at 2 story points, but you notice it involves modifying a shared authentication module used by 6 other services. How should you adjust?

choices.A: Double the estimate to 4 points because shared modules are always harder
choices.B: Reduce the estimate because shared modules are well-tested and stable
choices.C: Re-assess complexity upward because changes to shared modules require cross-service regression testing and coordination
choices.D: Accept the estimate since the developer knows the code best

questions.explanation: Shared module changes have hidden complexity: they require understanding downstream consumers, coordinating integration testing across 6 services, and managing deployment ordering. The original estimate likely only covers the code change itself.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of the test pyramid (many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest e2e tests)?

choices.A: It eliminates the need for manual QA entirely
choices.B: It minimizes total test execution time while maximizing defect detection at the cheapest level
choices.C: It ensures 100% code coverage at all levels
choices.D: It guarantees that integration bugs are always caught before deployment

questions.explanation: The test pyramid optimizes the cost-to-value ratio: unit tests are fast, cheap, and catch most logic bugs. Integration and e2e tests are slower and more expensive but catch different classes of defects. The pyramid shape maximizes coverage efficiency.

questions.question: When designing a CI/CD pipeline for a monorepo with 4 microservices, which approach avoids unnecessary builds?

choices.A: Build services on a rotating schedule regardless of changes
choices.B: Use path-based triggers so only services with changed files are built and deployed
choices.C: Let developers manually trigger builds for the services they changed
choices.D: Build all services on every commit to ensure consistency

questions.explanation: Path-based triggers (e.g., only build service-A when files under services/service-A/ change) prevent wasted CI resources and reduce pipeline duration, while still catching all relevant changes automatically.

questions.question: A code review reveals that a function has a cyclomatic complexity of 25. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Reject the PR entirely and reassign to a senior developer
choices.B: Request the author refactor into smaller functions, as high cyclomatic complexity correlates with increased defect rates and maintenance difficulty
choices.C: Add a comment documenting the complexity and approve
choices.D: Approve it if tests pass, since complexity metrics are unreliable

questions.explanation: Cyclomatic complexity of 25 means 25 independent paths through the code. This makes testing, understanding, and maintaining the function difficult. Refactoring into smaller, focused functions is the standard remediation.

questions.question: Which serialization format is most appropriate for high-frequency inter-service communication where schema evolution and bandwidth efficiency matter?

choices.A: JSON, because it is human-readable and universally supported
choices.B: Protocol Buffers, because they provide compact binary encoding with built-in schema evolution support
choices.C: XML, because it is the most widely supported format
choices.D: CSV, because it is the simplest format to parse

questions.explanation: Protocol Buffers offer compact binary serialization (5-10x smaller than JSON), built-in schema evolution via field numbering, and strong cross-language support, making them ideal for high-frequency inter-service communication.

questions.question: What is 'shift-left security' in the context of a development pipeline?

choices.A: Shifting security responsibilities from the security team to the leftmost column on the Kanban board
choices.B: Deploying security patches before feature releases
choices.C: Moving security testing earlier in the development lifecycle, ideally into the coding and CI phases
choices.D: Using left-aligned encryption algorithms for better performance

questions.explanation: Shift-left security means integrating security practices earlier in the SDLC (during coding, code review, and CI) rather than waiting for post-deployment security audits. This catches vulnerabilities when they are cheapest to fix.

questions.question: When should you prefer splitting a task into parallel subtasks versus keeping it as a single sequential task?

choices.A: Always split tasks to maximize developer utilization
choices.B: Never split tasks because integration overhead always exceeds the time savings
choices.C: Split when subtasks have minimal dependencies and can be developed, tested, and merged independently
choices.D: Split only when the task will take more than one sprint

questions.explanation: Task splitting is beneficial when subtasks have clear boundaries and minimal coupling, allowing parallel development without constant coordination. If subtasks are tightly coupled, the integration overhead can exceed the parallelism benefit.

questions.question: Which Docker best practice reduces image size and improves security?

choices.A: Installing all development tools in the production image for debugging
choices.B: Using multi-stage builds with a minimal runtime base image like Alpine or distroless
choices.C: Using the latest Ubuntu base image for all containers
choices.D: Running all processes as the root user for simplicity

questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds separate the build environment from the runtime environment. Using minimal base images like Alpine or distroless reduces attack surface and image size, often by 10-100x compared to full OS images.

questions.question: A request arrives to 'improve the search feature.' It is unclear whether this means backend query optimization, frontend UX improvements, or adding new search capabilities. What is the best first step?

choices.A: Decompose the request by gathering requirements to clarify scope before routing to any developer
choices.B: Route it to the frontend developer since the user mentioned the feature
choices.C: Assign it to the most senior developer on the team
choices.D: Route it to the backend developer since search is primarily a backend concern

questions.explanation: Ambiguous requests must be clarified before routing. Premature assignment wastes developer time on the wrong problem. Decomposing the request into specific, actionable requirements ensures correct routing and accurate estimation.

questions.question: Which deployment strategy allows you to test a new version with a small percentage of real production traffic before full rollout?

choices.A: Recreate deployment
choices.B: Rolling deployment
choices.C: Canary deployment
choices.D: Blue-green deployment

questions.explanation: Canary deployment routes a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version while the majority stays on the old version. This allows real-world validation before committing to a full rollout, unlike blue-green which switches 100% at once.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a code ownership model (e.g., CODEOWNERS file)?

choices.A: To track which developer has the highest commit count per module
choices.B: To ensure that changes to specific code areas are reviewed by developers with domain expertise in those areas
choices.C: To prevent any developer from modifying code they did not originally write
choices.D: To assign blame when bugs are discovered in production

questions.explanation: Code ownership models ensure that changes to critical or specialized areas are reviewed by developers who understand the domain context, catching issues that a general reviewer might miss. It is about review quality, not access restriction.

questions.question: When comparing Python and Go for a CPU-bound data processing pipeline, which statement is most accurate?

choices.A: Python is always faster because NumPy uses C under the hood
choices.B: Go and Python have equivalent performance for CPU-bound tasks
choices.C: Go will typically outperform Python for CPU-bound work due to compiled execution and true parallelism, though Python may be preferred if the team has strong NumPy/Pandas expertise
choices.D: Python's GIL makes it unsuitable for any parallel processing

questions.explanation: Go's compiled nature and goroutine-based parallelism give it a significant edge for CPU-bound work. However, Python's scientific computing ecosystem (NumPy, Pandas) can close the gap for specific data operations, making team expertise a valid factor.

questions.question: Which practice best addresses flaky tests in a CI pipeline?

choices.A: Automatically retry all failed tests 3 times and pass if any attempt succeeds
choices.B: Quarantine flaky tests into a separate suite, investigate root causes, and track flakiness metrics over time
choices.C: Increase test timeouts to 5 minutes to eliminate timing-related flakiness
choices.D: Delete flaky tests since they provide no reliable signal

questions.explanation: Quarantining separates flaky tests from the critical path so they do not block deployments, while tracking and root-cause analysis ensures they get fixed. Blind retries mask real failures; deletion loses coverage; timeouts only address one flakiness cause.

questions.question: What is the Eisenhower Matrix approach to priority management in software development?

choices.A: Assigning priorities based on which stakeholder requested the task
choices.B: Categorizing tasks by estimated completion time into four buckets
choices.C: Ranking all tasks by business revenue impact only
choices.D: Classifying tasks along two axes: urgency and importance, to determine whether to do now, schedule, delegate, or eliminate

questions.explanation: The Eisenhower Matrix classifies tasks as urgent/not-urgent and important/not-important. This helps coordinators avoid the trap of always working on urgent-but-unimportant tasks while neglecting important strategic work.

questions.question: When integrating SAST (Static Application Security Testing) into a CI pipeline, at which stage should it run?

choices.A: After deployment to production as a post-release check
choices.B: Only when a security team member manually triggers it
choices.C: During the build stage on every pull request, before code is merged to the main branch
choices.D: Only during the nightly build to avoid slowing down developer feedback

questions.explanation: SAST should run on every PR during the build stage to catch vulnerabilities before they enter the main codebase. This aligns with shift-left security and provides fast feedback to developers while the code context is fresh.

questions.question: A Kubernetes pod keeps crashing with OOMKilled status. What does this indicate?

choices.A: The pod could not connect to the network
choices.B: The container image was not found in the registry
choices.C: The pod's container exceeded its memory limit and was terminated by the kernel
choices.D: The pod's liveness probe failed

questions.explanation: OOMKilled means the container exceeded its configured memory limit (or the node ran out of memory). The Linux OOM killer terminates the process. The fix is either increasing the memory limit or fixing the memory leak in the application.

questions.question: What distinguishes a merge conflict resolution strategy based on 'ours' vs 'theirs'?

choices.A: 'Ours' and 'theirs' are identical but used for different git hosting platforms
choices.B: 'Ours' applies to binary files; 'theirs' applies to text files
choices.C: 'Ours' means the team lead decides; 'theirs' means the PR author decides
choices.D: 'Ours' keeps the current branch's changes and discards incoming; 'theirs' keeps incoming changes and discards current

questions.explanation: In git merge conflicts, 'ours' favors the current branch's version of conflicting changes, while 'theirs' favors the incoming branch's version. These strategies automate conflict resolution when one side's changes should always take priority.

questions.question: What is the primary cost of frequent context switching for developers?

choices.A: It has no measurable cost as long as tasks are well-documented
choices.B: It increases the time to reach productive flow state, with studies showing 15-25 minutes needed to regain deep focus after an interruption
choices.C: It only affects junior developers who cannot multitask
choices.D: It reduces the number of git commits per day

questions.explanation: Context switching destroys flow state. Research consistently shows that developers need 15-25 minutes to regain deep focus after an interruption. A coordinator should batch interruptions and protect focus time to maximize throughput.

questions.question: In an event-driven architecture, what problem does an event store solve that a traditional message broker alone does not?

choices.A: Event stores provide higher throughput than message brokers
choices.B: Event stores eliminate the need for consumers to acknowledge messages
choices.C: Event stores automatically scale horizontally without configuration
choices.D: Event stores persist the complete history of state changes, enabling temporal queries, replay, and rebuilding projections from scratch

questions.explanation: An event store maintains the full ordered history of events, enabling event replay, temporal queries, and projection rebuilding. A traditional broker typically deletes messages after consumption, losing this historical capability.

questions.question: A team has a Python backend developer, a React frontend developer, and a Go systems developer. A task requires building a CLI tool that parses large binary files and outputs structured JSON. Which routing decision is best?

choices.A: Route to the React developer since the output is JSON
choices.B: Split equally among all three developers
choices.C: Route to the Go developer since binary parsing and CLI tools align with systems programming expertise and Go's strong binary handling
choices.D: Route to the Python developer since Python excels at scripting tasks

questions.explanation: Parsing large binary files is a systems programming task where Go excels with its strong byte manipulation, static compilation (easy CLI distribution), and performance. The Go systems developer's specialization matches the task requirements.

questions.question: In the CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern, what is the fundamental reason for separating read and write models?

choices.A: To allow read and write models to be independently optimized, scaled, and evolved since their access patterns and consistency requirements differ fundamentally
choices.B: To ensure all queries return cached results
choices.C: To prevent SQL injection attacks on write operations
choices.D: To use different programming languages for reads and writes

questions.explanation: CQRS separates reads and writes because they have fundamentally different optimization needs: writes need transactional consistency and validation, while reads need denormalized views and caching. Separating them allows each to be tuned independently.

questions.question: When designing a code review checklist for a team, which approach yields the highest defect detection rate?

choices.A: Context-specific checklists tailored by change type (security-sensitive, performance-critical, API-breaking, etc.) with a common baseline
choices.B: An exhaustive checklist of 200+ items covering every possible issue
choices.C: A single generic checklist applied to all code changes regardless of type
choices.D: No checklist; rely entirely on reviewer expertise and intuition

questions.explanation: Context-specific checklists focus reviewer attention on the most relevant concerns for each change type, improving signal-to-noise. A common baseline catches universal issues, while tailored sections address type-specific risks without checklist fatigue.

questions.question: What is the strangler fig pattern in the context of legacy system migration?

choices.A: Running the legacy and modern systems in parallel indefinitely
choices.B: Rewriting the entire legacy system from scratch in a big-bang release
choices.C: Incrementally replacing legacy components by routing new functionality to a modern system while the old system continues to handle unchanged functionality, gradually strangling the old system
choices.D: Wrapping the legacy system in a container without changing any code

questions.explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces legacy functionality by intercepting calls at the edge and routing them to new implementations. Over time, more traffic flows to the new system until the legacy system can be decommissioned.

questions.question: When a CI pipeline takes 45 minutes per build, which optimization typically provides the largest improvement?

choices.A: Implementing layer caching for Docker builds, dependency caching, and parallelizing independent test suites
choices.B: Running the pipeline only during business hours
choices.C: Adding more RAM to the build server
choices.D: Switching from YAML-based to GUI-based pipeline configuration

questions.explanation: The three highest-impact CI optimizations are: caching (Docker layers, dependencies), parallelization (running independent test suites concurrently), and incremental builds. These typically reduce 45-minute pipelines to 10-15 minutes.

questions.question: In a polyglot microservices architecture with Python, Go, and TypeScript services, what is the best approach for defining API contracts between services?

choices.A: Use shared database tables as the integration contract
choices.B: Write all services in the same language to avoid contract issues
choices.C: Use a language-neutral Interface Definition Language (IDL) like OpenAPI or Protocol Buffers that generates client/server code for all target languages
choices.D: Let each service define its own ad-hoc JSON format

questions.explanation: A language-neutral IDL provides a single source of truth for API contracts, generates type-safe client and server stubs for each language, and catches contract violations at compile time rather than runtime.

questions.question: A developer's PR includes 1,200 lines of changes across 15 files. How should this be handled in code review?

choices.A: Request the developer break it into smaller, focused PRs because review effectiveness drops significantly beyond 200-400 lines of change
choices.B: Auto-approve it if the CI pipeline passes
choices.C: Review it as-is since the developer submitted it as a single unit of work
choices.D: Assign it to 5 reviewers so each reviews 3 files

questions.explanation: Research shows that review effectiveness drops sharply beyond 200-400 lines. A 1,200-line PR across 15 files will receive superficial review. Breaking it into focused PRs improves defect detection and reviewer engagement.

questions.question: When selecting between blue-green and canary deployment strategies, which factor most strongly favors canary?

choices.A: The infrastructure budget is extremely limited
choices.B: The application has stateful sessions that cannot be migrated
choices.C: The team wants instant rollback capability
choices.D: The team needs to validate performance characteristics under real production traffic patterns before full rollout

questions.explanation: Canary's key advantage is gradual exposure to real traffic, allowing validation of performance, error rates, and user experience with real data before committing. Blue-green switches all traffic at once, missing gradual validation.

questions.question: In hexagonal architecture (ports and adapters), what is the role of a 'port'?

choices.A: A network port number used for inter-service communication
choices.B: An interface that defines how the application core interacts with external systems, without specifying implementation details
choices.C: A database connection pool configuration
choices.D: A logging endpoint for monitoring

questions.explanation: In hexagonal architecture, a port is an interface (contract) that decouples the application core from external concerns. Adapters implement these ports for specific technologies (e.g., a PostgreSQL adapter implements a repository port).

questions.question: A task requires building a data pipeline that ingests CSV files, transforms data, and loads it into PostgreSQL. The team has a Python developer and a Go developer available. The pipeline needs to handle files up to 50GB. What is the best routing decision?

choices.A: Route to Python for the transform logic using streaming/chunked processing, since the data engineering ecosystem (pandas, SQLAlchemy, existing ETL libraries) reduces development time significantly
choices.B: Route to Python because pandas is the standard for data pipelines
choices.C: Split the task: Go for file reading, Python for transformation
choices.D: Route to Go because it handles large files better than Python in all cases

questions.explanation: Python's data engineering ecosystem (pandas chunked reading, SQLAlchemy, established ETL patterns) significantly reduces development time. For 50GB files, chunked/streaming processing in Python handles memory constraints. Go would offer raw speed but lack the ecosystem.

questions.question: What distinguishes feature flags from feature branches in terms of deployment risk?

choices.A: Feature flags are riskier because they add conditional logic to production code
choices.B: Feature flags eliminate all deployment risk
choices.C: They are identical in risk profile
choices.D: Feature branches are riskier because long-lived branches accumulate merge conflicts and integration risk; feature flags allow trunk-based development with runtime toggles

questions.explanation: Long-lived feature branches diverge from trunk, accumulating merge conflicts and delaying integration. Feature flags allow code to be merged to trunk behind toggles, reducing integration risk while controlling feature exposure at runtime.

questions.question: When assessing technical debt, which metric provides the most actionable insight for prioritization?

choices.A: Total lines of code in the repository
choices.B: The ratio of time spent on unplanned rework to planned feature work in areas with high change frequency
choices.C: The number of TODO comments in the codebase
choices.D: The age of the oldest file in the repository

questions.explanation: Technical debt is most impactful where it causes frequent rework in high-change areas. Measuring rework ratio in frequently modified code identifies where debt actively slows development, enabling targeted remediation with the highest ROI.

questions.question: In domain-driven design, what is a bounded context and why does it matter for task routing?

choices.A: A bounded context is an explicit boundary within which a domain model is defined and applicable; it matters for routing because developers should own complete bounded contexts, not fragments across multiple contexts
choices.B: A bounded context is a deployment unit; it matters for routing because each microservice is one context
choices.C: A bounded context is a security boundary; it matters for routing because it determines access control
choices.D: A bounded context is a database schema; it matters for routing because each developer owns one schema

questions.explanation: A bounded context defines where a specific domain model's terms and rules apply. Routing tasks within bounded context boundaries ensures developers understand the full domain model, reducing misunderstandings and cross-context coupling.

questions.question: When choosing between REST, gRPC, and GraphQL for a new API, which scenario most clearly favors gRPC?

choices.A: Internal service-to-service communication requiring low latency, strict contracts, and bidirectional streaming
choices.B: A mobile app that needs to fetch deeply nested data with a single request
choices.C: A public API consumed by third-party web applications
choices.D: A simple CRUD API with infrequent updates

questions.explanation: gRPC excels at internal service-to-service communication with its binary protocol (low latency), strict IDL contracts (proto files), bidirectional streaming, and auto-generated client stubs. REST is better for public APIs; GraphQL for flexible data fetching.

questions.question: What is the sidecar pattern in microservices, and when is it the correct choice?

choices.A: A helper container deployed alongside the main container in the same pod, handling cross-cutting concerns like logging, proxying, or TLS termination without modifying the main service
choices.B: A backup service that takes over when the primary service fails
choices.C: A service that monitors another service and restarts it when it crashes
choices.D: A pattern where two services share the same database to reduce latency

questions.explanation: The sidecar pattern deploys a helper container alongside the main application container (same pod in K8s). It handles cross-cutting concerns (mTLS, logging, metrics, proxy) without modifying the main service code, enabling polyglot infrastructure.

questions.question: A developer reports a skill gap in Kubernetes. As a coordinator, which assessment approach best determines the actual gap severity?

choices.A: Assign them a Kubernetes task and see if they succeed
choices.B: Ask the developer to rate their Kubernetes skills on a 1-10 scale
choices.C: Design a structured assessment: can they write a Deployment manifest, debug a CrashLoopBackOff, configure a HPA, set up RBAC? Map their responses to specific sub-skill gaps
choices.D: Check if they have a Kubernetes certification

questions.explanation: Structured skill assessment across specific sub-skills (manifests, debugging, scaling, security) identifies precise gaps. Self-ratings are unreliable, trial-by-fire is wasteful, and certifications do not map to practical capability.

questions.question: When should you choose serverless (e.g., AWS Lambda) over containerized services for a workload?

choices.A: Always, because serverless is cheaper and simpler
choices.B: When the workload requires persistent WebSocket connections
choices.C: When the workload is event-driven with variable load, short execution times (<15min), and the team wants to avoid infrastructure management overhead
choices.D: When the workload needs consistent sub-10ms cold start latency

questions.explanation: Serverless excels for event-driven, bursty workloads with short execution times where auto-scaling to zero saves cost. Persistent connections, long-running tasks, and strict cold-start requirements favor containers.

questions.question: In the context of dependency scanning in CI, what is the difference between SBOM generation and vulnerability scanning?

choices.A: SBOM generation creates an inventory of all software components and versions; vulnerability scanning checks that inventory against known CVE databases to identify security risks
choices.B: They are the same thing with different names
choices.C: Vulnerability scanning replaces the need for SBOMs
choices.D: SBOM is for open-source licenses; vulnerability scanning is for proprietary code

questions.explanation: An SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) inventories all components. Vulnerability scanning cross-references that inventory against CVE databases. Both are needed: the SBOM provides the what, vulnerability scanning provides the risk assessment.

questions.question: What is the key difference between integration tests and contract tests in a microservices environment?

choices.A: There is no meaningful difference; they test the same thing
choices.B: Contract tests are slower but more reliable than integration tests
choices.C: Integration tests are written by QA; contract tests are written by developers
choices.D: Integration tests verify that services work together end-to-end; contract tests verify that each service honors its API contract without requiring the other service to be running

questions.explanation: Contract tests (e.g., using Pact) verify API compatibility without requiring services to be running simultaneously, making them fast and reliable. Integration tests verify actual service interaction but are slower and more brittle.

questions.question: When comparing MessagePack and JSON for inter-service serialization, what is the primary tradeoff?

choices.A: MessagePack supports data types that JSON does not, such as functions
choices.B: MessagePack provides 30-50% size reduction and faster parsing compared to JSON, but sacrifices human readability and requires library support in all consuming languages
choices.C: MessagePack is human-readable while JSON is not
choices.D: JSON is always faster to parse than MessagePack

questions.explanation: MessagePack's binary format is 30-50% smaller and faster to parse than JSON, but it loses human readability (harder to debug) and requires MessagePack libraries in all services. JSON's ubiquity and readability often outweigh MessagePack's efficiency.

questions.question: A pipeline has three stages: build (3 min), unit tests (5 min), integration tests (12 min). Which optimization strategy best reduces total pipeline time?

choices.A: Run unit tests in parallel with the build, and parallelize integration test suites across multiple runners while implementing test result caching
choices.B: Skip integration tests on non-main branches
choices.C: Run all three stages sequentially but on a faster machine
choices.D: Move integration tests to a nightly build only

questions.explanation: Parallelizing independent stages and splitting the integration test suite across runners directly reduces wall-clock time. Test result caching skips unchanged tests. This preserves test coverage quality unlike skipping or deferring tests.

questions.question: In clean architecture, which layer should contain the business rules, and why?

choices.A: The presentation layer, because business rules determine what the user sees
choices.B: The database layer, because business rules should be enforced as close to the data as possible
choices.C: The use case (application) and entity (domain) layers, because business rules should be independent of frameworks, databases, and UI, enabling testability and portability
choices.D: The controller layer, because it receives user input and must validate it

questions.explanation: Clean architecture places business rules in the innermost layers (entities and use cases) with no dependencies on outer layers (UI, DB, frameworks). This makes rules testable in isolation and portable across infrastructure changes.

questions.question: When should a coordinator choose TDD (Test-Driven Development) over writing tests after implementation?

choices.A: Never, because TDD slows down development
choices.B: Always, because TDD is universally superior
choices.C: Only for trivial functions where the behavior is obvious
choices.D: When the requirements are well-defined and the domain logic is complex, since TDD enforces clear interface design and prevents scope creep

questions.explanation: TDD provides the most value when requirements are clear and logic is complex: it forces interface design before implementation, prevents scope creep, and creates comprehensive regression tests. Exploratory or prototyping work may benefit from test-after.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of using a shared database as an integration pattern between microservices?

choices.A: It creates tight coupling: schema changes in one service can break others, services cannot be independently deployed or scaled, and it violates bounded context boundaries
choices.B: It is too slow for microservices
choices.C: It prevents horizontal scaling of the database
choices.D: It requires all services to use the same programming language

questions.explanation: A shared database creates implicit coupling between services through the schema. Any schema change requires coordinated changes across all consumers, defeating the independent deployability that is the core benefit of microservices.

questions.question: When managing secrets in a CI/CD pipeline, which approach provides the strongest security posture?

choices.A: Use a dedicated secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) with short-lived credentials injected at runtime and audit logging
choices.B: Store secrets as environment variables in the CI configuration file
choices.C: Encode secrets in Base64 in the pipeline configuration
choices.D: Commit secrets to a private repository with encryption at rest

questions.explanation: A dedicated secrets manager provides centralized access control, rotation, audit logging, and short-lived credentials. Environment variables in CI config can leak in logs; committed secrets persist in git history; Base64 is not encryption.

questions.question: A team needs to choose between AWS, GCP, and Azure for a new project. The primary workload is ML training with GPU instances, and the team has strong Python and Kubernetes experience. Which factor should most influence the decision?

choices.A: The CEO's personal preference
choices.B: GPU instance availability, ML platform maturity (SageMaker vs Vertex AI vs Azure ML), and Kubernetes managed service quality (EKS vs GKE vs AKS) relative to team expertise
choices.C: Which provider offers the most free tier credits
choices.D: Which cloud provider has the most data centers globally

questions.explanation: For ML workloads, GPU availability and ML platform maturity are critical. GKE is widely considered the most mature managed Kubernetes (team's strength). The decision should be driven by workload-specific capabilities and team expertise alignment.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of infrastructure as code (IaC) over manual infrastructure provisioning?

choices.A: IaC makes infrastructure reproducible, version-controlled, reviewable, and testable, treating infrastructure changes with the same rigor as application code changes
choices.B: IaC eliminates all infrastructure costs
choices.C: IaC is always faster than clicking through a cloud console
choices.D: IaC prevents all security vulnerabilities

questions.explanation: IaC's primary advantage is treating infrastructure as a versioned, reviewable artifact. Changes go through PR review, can be tested in staging, and can be rolled back via git revert. Manual changes are unreproducible and unauditable.

questions.question: When Rust and Python are both options for a WebAssembly-targeted module, which language is typically better suited and why?

choices.A: Neither language supports WebAssembly compilation
choices.B: Python, because it has broader library support for WebAssembly
choices.C: Rust, because its lack of garbage collector and zero-cost abstractions produce compact, predictable WebAssembly modules with near-native performance
choices.D: Both produce identical WebAssembly output

questions.explanation: Rust's ownership model eliminates the need for a garbage collector runtime in the WASM output, producing smaller, faster modules. Python's runtime overhead and GC make it poorly suited for WASM targets compared to Rust.

questions.question: What test coverage percentage should a coordinator set as a team-wide minimum, and why?

choices.A: 0%, because coverage targets incentivize meaningless tests
choices.B: A pragmatic range like 70-80% for new code with focus on critical path coverage, because coverage past that point shows diminishing returns and can incentivize low-value tests
choices.C: 50%, because most bugs are in the other 50%
choices.D: 100%, because any uncovered code is a potential bug

questions.explanation: 70-80% is the empirical sweet spot where most critical logic is covered. Higher targets incentivize testing trivial getters/setters. The coordinator should emphasize critical path coverage and mutation testing quality over raw line coverage numbers.

questions.question: In a serverless architecture, what is the 'cold start' problem and how does it affect task routing decisions?

choices.A: Cold start is a database connection issue unrelated to serverless
choices.B: Cold start is the initialization latency when a serverless function is invoked after being idle, affecting routing decisions because latency-sensitive tasks should use provisioned concurrency or container-based alternatives
choices.C: Cold start only affects Python functions and is irrelevant for other languages
choices.D: Cold start is when a server room temperature drops, affecting hardware performance

questions.explanation: Cold starts (100ms-10s depending on runtime) occur when serverless platforms must initialize a new execution environment. Coordinators must route latency-sensitive workloads to pre-warmed instances or containers to avoid user-facing delays.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a security review gate in a CI/CD pipeline, and where should it be placed?

choices.A: An automated checkpoint after security scans (SAST, dependency scan, secrets detection) that blocks promotion to production if critical or high-severity findings exist
choices.B: A firewall rule that blocks deployments on weekends
choices.C: A weekly meeting where security engineers review all merged code
choices.D: A manual approval step placed after production deployment to verify security

questions.explanation: A security gate is an automated pipeline checkpoint that evaluates security scan results against a defined policy. Placed before production promotion, it blocks deployments with critical/high vulnerabilities, enforcing security standards without manual bottlenecks.

questions.question: When routing a task that requires implementing a GraphQL API, which developer skills are most critical to verify?

choices.A: REST API design experience, since GraphQL is just a wrapper around REST
choices.B: Frontend JavaScript experience only, since GraphQL is a frontend technology
choices.C: SQL database expertise, since GraphQL maps directly to SQL
choices.D: Schema design, resolver architecture, N+1 query prevention (DataLoader pattern), and understanding of query complexity limiting

questions.explanation: GraphQL has unique challenges: schema design drives the API contract, resolvers must avoid N+1 queries via DataLoader, and unconstrained queries need complexity limiting to prevent abuse. These skills are distinct from REST expertise.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using environment-per-PR (ephemeral preview environments) in a CI/CD pipeline?

choices.A: It reduces cloud infrastructure costs
choices.B: It speeds up the CI build process
choices.C: It eliminates the need for staging environments permanently
choices.D: It allows QA, product, and design stakeholders to review changes in an isolated, production-like environment before merging, catching integration issues earlier

questions.explanation: Ephemeral PR environments enable stakeholders to interact with changes in isolation before merge. This catches UX, integration, and configuration issues earlier in the workflow, reducing post-merge defects and review cycle time.

questions.question: In a monolith-to-microservices migration, which decomposition strategy is recommended as a first step?

choices.A: Split the monolith by database tables, one service per table
choices.B: Extract all entities into separate services simultaneously
choices.C: Rewrite the entire monolith in a new language
choices.D: Identify the bounded context with the highest change frequency and most independent data ownership, extract it first as a proof of concept

questions.explanation: Starting with the highest-change-frequency bounded context maximizes learning while delivering value. Its frequent changes justify the investment, and independent data ownership reduces the extraction complexity. This proves the pattern before wider decomposition.

questions.question: When managing artifact versions in a CI pipeline, what is the best practice for ensuring reproducible deployments?

choices.A: Use 'latest' tags for all Docker images to always deploy the newest version
choices.B: Store artifacts on the build server's local disk
choices.C: Tag artifacts with the git commit SHA or a semantic version pinned to a specific build, and use immutable artifact repositories where published versions cannot be overwritten
choices.D: Rebuild artifacts from source for every deployment

questions.explanation: Immutable, SHA-tagged artifacts ensure that what was tested is exactly what is deployed. 'Latest' tags are mutable and non-deterministic. Rebuilding introduces drift risk. Local storage is fragile and non-distributed.

questions.question: What characteristic of Rust makes it uniquely suited for tasks where both memory safety and zero-overhead abstractions are requirements?

choices.A: Its garbage collector is faster than Go's
choices.B: Its ownership and borrowing system enforces memory safety at compile time without runtime overhead, eliminating both garbage collection pauses and manual memory management bugs
choices.C: Its dynamic typing allows flexible memory management
choices.D: Its interpreter optimizes memory allocation at runtime

questions.explanation: Rust's ownership system is unique: it prevents memory bugs (use-after-free, data races) at compile time, not runtime. This means no GC pauses (unlike Go/Java) and no manual memory management bugs (unlike C/C++), achieving safety with zero overhead.

questions.question: When is BDD (Behavior-Driven Development) a better choice than TDD for a project?

choices.A: When the team needs a shared understanding of requirements with non-technical stakeholders, and specifications should serve as both documentation and executable tests
choices.B: When the codebase is entirely backend with no user-facing features
choices.C: When the team wants to minimize the number of tests written
choices.D: When the project has no non-technical stakeholders

questions.explanation: BDD's Given/When/Then format creates a shared language between technical and non-technical stakeholders. The specifications become living documentation that is also executable, bridging the gap between requirements and implementation.

questions.question: What is the primary security risk of using DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) as the only security testing method?

choices.A: DAST cannot test web applications
choices.B: DAST produces too many false positives to be useful
choices.C: DAST only tests the running application from the outside, missing code-level vulnerabilities like hardcoded secrets, insecure deserialization patterns, and logic flaws that are visible in source code
choices.D: DAST is too slow to run in CI pipelines

questions.explanation: DAST tests from the outside (like an attacker), finding runtime vulnerabilities but missing code-level issues. SAST analyzes source code and finds hardcoded secrets, insecure patterns, and logic flaws. Both are needed for comprehensive coverage.

questions.question: When designing a Kubernetes deployment for a stateful application (e.g., a database), which resource type is most appropriate?

choices.A: Job, because databases are batch workloads
choices.B: Deployment, because all K8s workloads should use Deployments
choices.C: DaemonSet, because it ensures one instance per node
choices.D: StatefulSet, because it provides stable network identities, ordered deployment/scaling, and persistent volume claims that survive pod restarts

questions.explanation: StatefulSets provide stable pod identities (pod-0, pod-1), ordered startup/shutdown, and persistent volume claims that reattach to the same pod. These properties are essential for databases and other stateful workloads that Deployments cannot guarantee.

questions.question: A coordinator receives three tasks simultaneously: a production hotfix for a payment bug, a feature requested by the CEO for a demo tomorrow, and a tech debt refactoring that would unblock 3 other tasks. How should these be prioritized?

choices.A: Production hotfix first (revenue impact and user trust), then tech debt (unblocks 3 tasks giving highest throughput), then CEO demo (deadline-driven scheduling after critical items)
choices.B: Tech debt first (unblocks the most work), then hotfix, then demo
choices.C: All three in parallel with equal priority
choices.D: CEO demo first (highest authority), then hotfix, then tech debt

questions.explanation: The production payment bug has immediate revenue and trust impact. Tech debt unblocking 3 tasks has the highest throughput multiplier. The CEO demo has a fixed deadline but lower severity. This ordering maximizes value delivery while respecting urgency.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the circuit breaker pattern in microservices?

choices.A: To compress network traffic between services
choices.B: To balance load across multiple service instances
choices.C: To encrypt communication between services
choices.D: To prevent cascading failures by detecting when a downstream service is unhealthy and failing fast instead of queuing requests that will timeout

questions.explanation: The circuit breaker monitors downstream call failures. When failures exceed a threshold, it 'opens' and returns errors immediately instead of waiting for timeouts. This prevents thread pool exhaustion and cascading failures across the service mesh.

questions.question: When choosing an IaC tool, what distinguishes Terraform from Pulumi as a selection criterion?

choices.A: Terraform uses a declarative DSL (HCL) with a large provider ecosystem, while Pulumi uses general-purpose languages (Python, TypeScript, Go), making the choice depend on team preference for DSLs vs familiar programming languages
choices.B: Terraform does not support state management while Pulumi does
choices.C: Terraform can only manage AWS resources while Pulumi supports all clouds
choices.D: Pulumi is faster at provisioning than Terraform in all cases

questions.explanation: The core distinction is DSL vs general-purpose language. Terraform's HCL is purpose-built and widely adopted. Pulumi allows IaC in Python/TypeScript/Go, which can be better for teams who prefer familiar languages and need complex logic in their IaC.

questions.question: A team has 5 developers receiving an average of 8 PRs per day. What strategy prevents code review from becoming a bottleneck?

choices.A: Assign all PRs to the tech lead for centralized review
choices.B: Implement automated review for style/linting, use CODEOWNERS for domain-specific routing, set a 4-hour SLA for first review response, and rotate review responsibilities to spread knowledge
choices.C: Batch all reviews to a single hour each day
choices.D: Skip code review for small PRs under 50 lines

questions.explanation: Automating style checks removes noise from human review. CODEOWNERS routes to domain experts. SLAs prevent staleness. Rotation spreads knowledge and prevents single-person bottlenecks. Together these maintain quality without blocking velocity.

questions.question: What is the key difference between eventual consistency and strong consistency, and how does it influence architecture decisions?

choices.A: Strong consistency is only available in SQL databases
choices.B: They are the same; all distributed systems are strongly consistent
choices.C: Eventual consistency is a bug; strong consistency is the correct behavior
choices.D: Strong consistency guarantees every read returns the latest write; eventual consistency guarantees reads will converge over time. Strong consistency requires coordination (higher latency), while eventual consistency allows higher availability and throughput

questions.explanation: Per the CAP theorem, strong consistency requires cross-node coordination (sacrificing availability or latency). Eventual consistency trades immediate accuracy for availability and throughput. Architecture decisions depend on which tradeoff the domain tolerates.

questions.question: When setting up cost optimization for cloud workloads, which approach typically yields the largest savings?

choices.A: Turning off all development environments permanently
choices.B: Right-sizing instances based on actual utilization metrics, using reserved instances or savings plans for predictable workloads, and auto-scaling for variable workloads
choices.C: Switching to a cheaper cloud provider
choices.D: Using only spot instances for all workloads

questions.explanation: Right-sizing eliminates waste from over-provisioned instances. Reserved capacity discounts (30-60% savings) apply to predictable baselines. Auto-scaling handles variable load efficiently. This three-pronged approach typically yields 40-60% cost reduction.

questions.question: When a coordinator notices that a developer consistently underestimates task complexity, what is the most effective intervention?

choices.A: Introduce calibration through post-task retrospectives comparing estimates to actuals, identify systematic bias patterns (e.g., underestimating integration work), and pair with an experienced estimator for complex tasks
choices.B: Remove them from estimation responsibilities
choices.C: Secretly double all their estimates
choices.D: Add a fixed 50% buffer to all tasks

questions.explanation: Calibration through data (estimate vs actual) reveals specific bias patterns. Pairing transfers estimation skills. This develops the developer's judgment rather than working around it, producing long-term improvement.

questions.question: In a Python/TypeScript polyglot system, which approach best handles shared data validation logic?

choices.A: Use a Python-to-TypeScript transpiler
choices.B: Define validation schemas in a language-neutral format (JSON Schema, OpenAPI) and generate validators for both languages, ensuring a single source of truth
choices.C: Only validate in the backend; trust the frontend
choices.D: Duplicate the validation code in both languages independently

questions.explanation: Language-neutral schemas (JSON Schema, OpenAPI) provide a single source of truth that generates type-safe validators for both languages. This eliminates drift between frontend/backend validation and reduces duplication bugs.

questions.question: When a developer reports that their tests pass locally but fail in CI, what is the most likely category of root cause?

choices.A: The CI server has a bug in its test runner
choices.B: Network latency between the CI server and the code repository
choices.C: Environment differences: implicit local dependencies (running services, environment variables, file system state, timezone, OS libraries) that are not captured in the CI configuration
choices.D: The developer is lying about local test results

questions.explanation: Local-pass/CI-fail almost always stems from environment differences: locally running services (Redis, DB), undeclared environment variables, filesystem assumptions (paths, permissions), or OS-level differences. The fix is making CI fully deterministic via containers.

questions.question: What is the role of an API gateway in a microservices architecture, and when is it overkill?

choices.A: An API gateway handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing, TLS) at the edge; it is overkill for internal-only service meshes with fewer than 3 services where a simple reverse proxy suffices
choices.B: An API gateway is always necessary for any microservices system
choices.C: An API gateway replaces the need for service-level authentication
choices.D: An API gateway is only useful for GraphQL APIs

questions.explanation: API gateways centralize edge concerns, reducing duplication across services. However, for simple internal architectures, a lightweight reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik) handles routing without the operational complexity of a full gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway).

questions.question:
You coordinate a team with three developers:
- Dev A: Python expert, intermediate Go, no React experience, currently at 70% capacity
- Dev B: React/TypeScript expert, intermediate Python, no Go experience, currently at 40% capacity
- Dev C: Go expert, intermediate React, no Python experience, currently at 90% capacity

A new feature requires: (1) a Go gRPC service with complex binary protocol handling, (2) a Python data transformation pipeline, and (3) a React dashboard consuming both services.

Step 1: Identify the optimal task-to-developer mapping considering both expertise and load.
Step 2: Determine the correct sequencing given data dependencies.
Step 3: Select the best approach.

Which plan is correct?

choices.A: Dev A takes Go gRPC (intermediate Go), Dev C takes React (intermediate React), Dev B takes Python (intermediate Python). This spreads growth opportunities evenly.
choices.B: Dev B takes all three since they have the most free capacity at 40%
choices.C: All three developers collaborate on each component sequentially to maximize knowledge sharing
choices.D: Dev C takes Go gRPC, Dev A takes Python pipeline, Dev B takes React dashboard. Sequence: Go gRPC and Python pipeline in parallel, then React dashboard. Dev C's high load is acceptable because the Go task is their strongest skill.

questions.explanation: Expertise-first routing: Dev C handles the complex Go binary protocol (expert), Dev A handles Python transforms (expert), Dev B builds the React dashboard (expert). The dashboard depends on both APIs, so backend services run in parallel first. Dev C's 90% load is manageable for their strongest skill.

questions.question:
Your team is migrating a monolithic e-commerce application to microservices. The monolith handles: user auth, product catalog, shopping cart, order processing, payment, and notification services. The codebase has tight coupling between cart and order processing (shared database tables), and between payment and notification (synchronous calls).

Step 1: Identify which bounded contexts can be extracted independently.
Step 2: Determine the extraction order that minimizes risk.
Step 3: Choose the migration strategy.

Which approach is correct?

choices.A: Extract user auth and product catalog first (independent, read-heavy, no tight coupling), then notification (decoupled from payment via async events), then cart + orders together (coupled), and finally payment (highest risk, extracted last with most experience). Use strangler fig pattern throughout.
choices.B: Extract cart and orders separately despite their shared database tables
choices.C: Extract all 6 services simultaneously using a big-bang rewrite
choices.D: Start with payment extraction since it is the most critical service

questions.explanation: Auth and catalog are natural first candidates: independent, read-heavy, low-risk. Notification can decouple from payment via async events. Cart/orders must move together due to shared state. Payment goes last because it has the highest risk and the team will have the most migration experience by then.

questions.question:
You discover that your CI/CD pipeline has the following security issues:
1. Docker images are built using root user
2. npm packages are installed without integrity checking
3. Database credentials are passed as build arguments
4. No vulnerability scanning on third-party dependencies
5. The deployment script has write access to all production servers

Step 1: Categorize each issue by severity.
Step 2: Determine the remediation priority.
Step 3: Select the response plan.

Which plan correctly addresses all issues in the right order?

choices.A: Fix Docker root user first since containers are the most visible component
choices.B: Only fix the credential issue; the others are low risk
choices.C: Fix all 5 issues simultaneously in a single sprint
choices.D: Priority 1 (Critical): Move database credentials to a secrets manager (issue 3) and restrict deployment script access with least-privilege IAM (issue 5). Priority 2 (High): Add dependency vulnerability scanning (issue 4) and enable npm integrity checking (issue 2). Priority 3 (Medium): Switch Docker builds to non-root user (issue 1).

questions.explanation: Credentials as build args (visible in image layers and logs) and over-privileged deployment (blast radius) are critical. Dependency scanning and integrity checking prevent supply-chain attacks (high). Non-root Docker is defense-in-depth (medium). This ordering addresses the highest-impact risks first.

questions.question:
A Python service processes 10,000 webhook events per minute. Under load, response times spike to 30 seconds and the service drops events. Investigation reveals:
- Each webhook handler makes 3 synchronous HTTP calls to downstream services (avg 200ms each)
- The PostgreSQL connection pool is limited to 10 connections
- The service runs in a single-process WSGI configuration
- No message queue is used; events are processed inline

Step 1: Identify the primary bottleneck.
Step 2: Determine the correct combination of fixes.
Step 3: Select the best response.

Which approach resolves the core issues?

choices.A: Rewrite the entire service in Go for better performance
choices.B: Increase the PostgreSQL connection pool to 1000 connections
choices.C: Add a CDN in front of the webhook endpoint
choices.D: Switch to an async framework (FastAPI/ASGI), introduce a message queue (Redis/RabbitMQ) to decouple webhook receipt from processing, increase the connection pool to 50-100 connections, and run multiple worker processes

questions.explanation: The primary bottleneck is synchronous processing: 3x200ms = 600ms per event, single-process WSGI with 10 DB connections caps at ~16 events/second. Async processing, message queue for decoupling, larger connection pool, and multi-worker deployment address all bottlenecks without a full rewrite.

questions.question:
You are designing a test strategy for a new microservices platform with 8 services. The team has 3 QA engineers and 12 developers. Services communicate via REST APIs and an event bus.

Step 1: Define the test pyramid layers and ownership.
Step 2: Identify the critical integration boundaries.
Step 3: Select the strategy that maximizes coverage with the available team.

Which strategy is optimal?

choices.A: Write unit tests only and rely on production monitoring to catch integration issues
choices.B: QA engineers write all tests; developers focus only on code
choices.C: Developers own unit tests (80%+ coverage) and contract tests (Pact) for their service boundaries. QA engineers own integration test suites for critical cross-service flows, e2e smoke tests, and exploratory testing. Contract tests verify API and event bus compatibility without requiring all services to be running.
choices.D: Only write e2e tests since they cover everything

questions.explanation: This divides ownership by expertise: developers understand their service internals (unit + contract tests), QA understands cross-cutting flows (integration + e2e + exploratory). Contract tests (Pact) verify service compatibility without running the full stack, making them fast and reliable for 8-service architectures.

questions.question:
Your organization runs a polyglot system: Python ML service, Go API gateway, TypeScript frontend, Rust data processing engine. A bug report indicates data corruption between the Go API and Rust processor.

Step 1: Identify likely serialization/deserialization failure points.
Step 2: Determine the diagnostic approach.
Step 3: Select the fix that prevents future cross-language data issues.

Which approach is correct?

choices.A: Add more logging to both services and wait for the bug to happen again
choices.B: Switch all serialization to XML for better debugging
choices.C: Audit the serialization contract: check for integer overflow differences (Go's int vs Rust's i64), floating point handling, null/None/nil semantics, UTF-8 string handling, and endianness. Implement a shared Protocol Buffers schema as the canonical contract, add cross-language integration tests that verify serialization round-trips, and deploy contract testing in CI.
choices.D: Rewrite the Rust service in Go to eliminate the language boundary

questions.explanation: Cross-language data corruption typically stems from type system mismatches (integer widths, float precision, null handling). A shared IDL (protobuf) eliminates ambiguity. Cross-language round-trip tests in CI prevent regression. This addresses root cause rather than symptoms.

questions.question:
You need to design a CI/CD pipeline for a monorepo containing 6 microservices, a shared library, and infrastructure-as-code. Requirements: independent service deployments, shared library changes trigger dependent service rebuilds, infrastructure changes require approval, and the pipeline must complete in under 15 minutes.

Step 1: Design the dependency graph and trigger rules.
Step 2: Optimize for the 15-minute constraint.
Step 3: Select the correct pipeline design.

Which design meets all requirements?

choices.A: Path-based triggers with a dependency graph: changes to shared library trigger builds for all 6 dependent services. Each service has an independent build-test-deploy pipeline. Infrastructure changes trigger a separate plan-approve-apply pipeline with manual approval gate. All service builds parallelized across runners. Docker layer caching and test result caching reduce build times.
choices.B: A single sequential pipeline that builds services one at a time
choices.C: Separate repositories for each service to avoid the dependency problem
choices.D: Build everything on every commit to ensure consistency

questions.explanation: Path-based triggers with dependency awareness ensures only affected services rebuild. Parallel runners keep wall-clock time under 15 minutes. Separate IaC pipeline with approval gates addresses infrastructure safety. Caching prevents redundant work across frequent commits.

questions.question:
A startup is choosing between three architecture options for a new SaaS product:
Option X: Monolith (Django, PostgreSQL)
Option Y: Microservices from day 1 (Go services, Kafka, Kubernetes)
Option Z: Modular monolith (FastAPI with clearly bounded modules, PostgreSQL with schema-per-module, designed for future extraction)

The team has 4 developers, needs to launch MVP in 3 months, and expects to scale to 10,000 users in year one.

Step 1: Evaluate each option against team size, timeline, and scale requirements.
Step 2: Assess operational overhead.
Step 3: Select the best option.

Which option is most appropriate?

choices.A: Option X: Django monolith because it is the simplest
choices.B: Option Z: Modular monolith, because it combines fast development velocity for 4 developers with clear module boundaries that enable future microservice extraction, avoids distributed systems complexity during MVP, and handles 10K users easily on a single deployment
choices.C: A hybrid: some modules as microservices, others as monolith
choices.D: Option Y: Microservices from day 1 because the product needs to scale

questions.explanation: With 4 developers and a 3-month timeline, microservices create premature operational complexity. A pure monolith risks future extraction difficulty. The modular monolith provides MVP velocity with intentional boundaries (schema-per-module) that make future extraction possible when scale demands it. 10K users is well within monolith capacity.

questions.question:
Your team discovers that a production Kubernetes cluster has these issues:
1. All pods run as root
2. No network policies (all pods can communicate with all others)
3. Secrets are stored as plain base64 in etcd (no encryption at rest)
4. No resource limits on any deployment
5. Container images are pulled from a public registry with no signature verification

Step 1: Rank these by exploitation severity.
Step 2: Determine which can be fixed without downtime.
Step 3: Select the correct remediation plan.

Which remediation plan is correct?

choices.A: Fix all issues simultaneously in a maintenance window
choices.B: Only fix the root user issue since it is the most commonly cited K8s vulnerability
choices.C: Immediate (no downtime): Enable etcd encryption at rest (issue 3) and add network policies with default-deny (issue 2). Rolling update: Add securityContext with non-root and readOnlyRootFilesystem (issue 1), set resource limits (issue 4). Pipeline change: Add image signature verification and private registry mirroring (issue 5).
choices.D: Migrate to a new cluster with all fixes applied

questions.explanation: Etcd encryption and network policies can be applied without pod restarts (highest severity, no downtime). Security contexts and resource limits require rolling updates (planned). Image verification is a pipeline change (prevents future risk). This ordering minimizes disruption while addressing highest-risk items first.

questions.question:
You are coordinating a complex feature that requires:
- Backend: New REST endpoints in Python (3 days estimated)
- Frontend: React components with complex state management (4 days estimated)
- Database: Schema migration adding 3 new tables with foreign keys (1 day estimated)
- Infrastructure: New Redis cache cluster deployment (1 day estimated)

Dev A (Python) and Dev B (React) are available. You handle infrastructure. The feature must ship in 5 business days.

Step 1: Identify the critical path.
Step 2: Determine which tasks can parallelize.
Step 3: Select the correct execution plan.

Which plan meets the 5-day deadline?

choices.A: Dev A does everything sequentially while Dev B waits
choices.B: Sequential: DB migration -> Backend -> Frontend -> Infrastructure
choices.C: Ship without the database migration to meet the deadline
choices.D: Day 1: DB migration (Dev A) + Redis deployment (you) in parallel. Days 1-3: Backend development (Dev A) starts after migration. Days 1-4: Frontend development (Dev B) starts day 1 using mocked API contracts. Day 4-5: Integration and testing. Critical path is frontend at 4 days, so Dev B starts immediately with agreed API contracts.

questions.explanation: The critical path is frontend (4 days). By starting Dev B immediately with API contract mocks, the 4-day frontend work overlaps with backend. DB migration and Redis deploy on day 1 (parallel, no dependency). Integration days 4-5 catch issues. Total: 5 days.

questions.question:
A legacy Node.js service (Express, callbacks, no TypeScript) needs modernization. The service handles 2,000 req/s and has 85% test coverage. Options:
A) Incremental migration to TypeScript with async/await, keep Express
B) Full rewrite in Go
C) Migrate to NestJS (TypeScript) with a strangler fig approach
D) Add TypeScript types via JSDoc comments only

Step 1: Evaluate risk vs benefit for each option at 2,000 req/s with existing test coverage.
Step 2: Consider team disruption and rollback capability.
Step 3: Select the best modernization path.

Which option balances risk and modernization benefit?

choices.A: Option C: NestJS with strangler fig, because it provides a modern framework with TypeScript safety, the strangler fig approach allows incremental migration with rollback capability per route, and the 85% test coverage enables confident refactoring
choices.B: Option B: Full rewrite in Go because it will handle more traffic
choices.C: Option A: Keep Express because changing frameworks is too risky
choices.D: Option D: JSDoc only because it is the lowest risk

questions.explanation: NestJS provides TypeScript, dependency injection, and modern patterns. The strangler fig approach migrates route-by-route with rollback per endpoint. The 85% test coverage validates each migration step. A full rewrite risks the Big Rewrite failure mode; JSDoc adds types without structural improvement.

questions.question:
You are designing a review process for a security-critical financial API. Current state: 2 reviewers per PR, no specialized security review, automated linting only.

Step 1: Identify what the current process misses for financial APIs.
Step 2: Design the enhanced review pipeline.
Step 3: Select the correct review process.

Which process provides adequate security for financial code?

choices.A: Increase to 3 general reviewers per PR
choices.B: Replace human review with AI code review tools
choices.C: Add automated SAST and dependency scanning as pre-merge gates. Require 2 general reviewers plus 1 mandatory security reviewer for any PR touching auth, payment, or PII handling code paths. Security reviewer checks: input validation, authorization checks, audit logging, encryption, and SQL injection prevention. Add a security-specific checklist triggered by CODEOWNERS path matching.
choices.D: Only review PRs that touch the payment module; trust other code

questions.explanation: Financial APIs need layered security review: automated scanning catches known vulnerability patterns, general reviewers verify logic, and a specialized security reviewer checks domain-specific concerns (auth, encryption, audit trails). CODEOWNERS automates routing to ensure security-sensitive paths always get expert review.

questions.question:
Your system uses event-driven architecture with Kafka. You discover three problems:
1. Consumer lag is growing (consumers cannot keep up with producers)
2. Events are sometimes processed out of order, causing data inconsistency
3. A consumer crash causes duplicate event processing on restart

Step 1: Identify the root cause of each problem.
Step 2: Determine whether these are configuration, architecture, or code issues.
Step 3: Select the combined fix.

Which approach addresses all three problems?

choices.A: Increase Kafka partition count to fix all three issues
choices.B: Add retry logic with exponential backoff to fix all three issues
choices.C: For lag (1): Increase consumer instances and partition count for parallel consumption. For ordering (2): Use partition keys to ensure related events route to the same partition, maintaining per-entity ordering. For duplicates (3): Implement idempotent consumers using event ID deduplication tables and transactional outbox pattern for exactly-once semantics.
choices.D: Switch from Kafka to a traditional REST-based architecture

questions.explanation: Lag is a throughput issue (scale consumers + partitions). Ordering is a partitioning issue (key-based partition routing). Duplicates are an idempotency issue (deduplication + transactional outbox). Each problem has a distinct root cause requiring a specific solution.

questions.question:
A team needs to choose between three database options for a new service:
- PostgreSQL (relational, strong consistency, complex queries)
- MongoDB (document store, flexible schema, horizontal scaling)
- Redis (in-memory, sub-millisecond reads, limited query capability)

The service requirements: user session management, 50,000 concurrent sessions, sub-5ms read latency, session data is semi-structured (varies by user type), sessions expire after 30 minutes.

Step 1: Evaluate each option against the requirements.
Step 2: Consider operational complexity.
Step 3: Select the best option.

Which database is the best fit?

choices.A: Redis, because sub-5ms latency requires in-memory storage, TTL natively handles 30-minute expiration, semi-structured data fits Redis hashes or JSON module, and 50K concurrent sessions is well within Redis capacity. Use PostgreSQL as a secondary store only if long-term session analytics are needed.
choices.B: Use all three in a tiered architecture
choices.C: PostgreSQL because it supports complex queries on session data
choices.D: MongoDB because the semi-structured data matches its document model

questions.explanation: The requirements strongly favor Redis: sub-5ms latency demands in-memory storage, TTL is built-in for expiration, Redis hashes handle semi-structured data, and 50K sessions is modest for Redis. PostgreSQL and MongoDB introduce unnecessary disk I/O latency for this use case.

questions.question:
You are coordinating a zero-downtime migration from AWS to GCP for a production system. The system includes: 3 microservices on ECS, RDS PostgreSQL, S3 for file storage, CloudFront CDN, and Route 53 DNS.

Step 1: Identify components that can be migrated independently.
Step 2: Determine the migration sequence that maintains availability.
Step 3: Select the correct migration plan.

Which plan ensures zero downtime?

choices.A: Only migrate the database; keep services on AWS
choices.B: Migrate DNS first, then services, then database
choices.C: Migrate everything simultaneously during a maintenance window
choices.D: Phase 1: Set up GCP equivalents (GKE, Cloud SQL, GCS, Cloud CDN) and establish data replication (AWS RDS -> Cloud SQL, S3 -> GCS sync). Phase 2: Deploy services to GKE with traffic still on AWS. Phase 3: Shift DNS (Route 53 -> Cloud DNS) with weighted routing, gradually moving traffic from AWS to GCP. Phase 4: Verify all traffic on GCP, decommission AWS. Rollback at any phase by shifting DNS weight back.

questions.explanation: The phased approach with data replication establishes GCP as a parallel environment before any traffic moves. Weighted DNS routing enables gradual traffic shift with instant rollback capability. Data replication ensures consistency during the transition. No single point of failure.

questions.question:
Your team's integration test suite has grown to 2,000 tests taking 90 minutes. Developers skip running them locally, and CI feedback is too slow. Analysis shows:
- 300 tests are end-to-end (browser-based), averaging 15s each
- 1,200 tests hit a real database, averaging 2s each
- 500 tests make real HTTP calls to external APIs, averaging 3s each

Step 1: Identify which test categories to optimize and how.
Step 2: Calculate the expected time savings.
Step 3: Select the best optimization plan.

Which plan provides the most improvement?

choices.A: Replace real HTTP calls with contract tests and WireMock stubs (500 tests: 3s -> 0.1s, saving ~24 min). Parallelize database tests across 4 shards (1200 tests: 40 min -> 10 min, saving 30 min). Parallelize e2e tests across 6 browser instances (300 tests: 75 min -> 12.5 min, saving 62.5 min). Total: 90 min -> ~23 min.
choices.B: Convert all tests to unit tests by removing external dependencies
choices.C: Delete the 300 e2e tests since they take the longest per test
choices.D: Run all tests in parallel on a single machine with more CPU cores

questions.explanation: This approach targets each category with the right optimization: contract tests + stubs for HTTP calls (eliminate network latency), sharded parallel runs for DB tests, and multi-browser parallelism for e2e. The calculated result (~23 min) is achievable and preserves test quality. A single machine cannot parallelize browser tests effectively.

questions.question:
A request comes in to build a real-time analytics dashboard. Requirements:
- Ingest 100,000 events/second from 5 data sources
- Sub-second query latency on aggregated data
- Support time-series queries, top-N, and percentile calculations
- Data retention: raw events for 7 days, aggregated data for 1 year
- Team has Python and TypeScript expertise only

Step 1: Select the appropriate data pipeline architecture.
Step 2: Choose the query/storage engine.
Step 3: Select the best technology stack.

Which stack is most appropriate?

choices.A: Kafka for event ingestion, ClickHouse (or TimescaleDB) for time-series storage and sub-second aggregation queries, a Python stream processor (Faust or Kafka Streams equivalent) for real-time aggregation, and a TypeScript/React frontend with WebSocket for live updates. Separate hot (7-day raw) and cold (1-year aggregated) storage tiers.
choices.B: PostgreSQL with materialized views for everything
choices.C: Store all events in Redis for maximum speed
choices.D: Use a SaaS analytics tool like Mixpanel instead of building

questions.explanation: At 100K events/s, Kafka handles ingestion reliably. ClickHouse/TimescaleDB are purpose-built for time-series analytics with sub-second aggregation. Python stream processing fits team expertise. Hot/cold tiering manages the retention requirements cost-effectively. The team can build this with their Python + TypeScript skills.

questions.question:
You are designing a rollback strategy for a microservices deployment where Service A v2 introduces a breaking API change consumed by Services B and C. All three services must be updated.

Step 1: Identify the deployment ordering constraint.
Step 2: Design the rollback safety mechanism.
Step 3: Select the correct strategy.

Which deployment and rollback strategy is safest?

choices.A: Use API versioning: Deploy Service A v2 with both v1 and v2 endpoints active. Deploy Services B and C updated to use v2 endpoints behind feature flags. Gradually enable v2 consumption. If issues arise, disable feature flags (instant rollback to v1 endpoints). After validation, deprecate v1 endpoints. Each service can be rolled back independently.
choices.B: Deploy B and C first (they will error on the v1 API) then deploy A
choices.C: Deploy Service A v2 first, then immediately update B and C
choices.D: Deploy all three simultaneously and roll back all three if anything fails

questions.explanation: API versioning with parallel endpoints eliminates the coordination problem: Service A serves both versions simultaneously, consumers switch via feature flags. Rollback is instant (flag toggle). No service depends on a specific deployment order. This is the expand-contract (parallel change) pattern.

questions.question:
You are reviewing a PR that introduces a new caching layer. The implementation uses an in-memory cache (dictionary) with no expiration, no size limit, and no cache invalidation strategy. The cache stores user profile objects.

Step 1: Identify all issues with this implementation.
Step 2: Assess the production risk of each issue.
Step 3: Select the correct review feedback.

Which review feedback is most complete?
