choices.A: Reject and ask them to use Redis instead, without explaining why
choices.B: Approve the PR since the tests pass
choices.C: Request adding a TTL and approve
choices.D: Request changes addressing: (1) Memory leak risk: unbounded cache will grow until OOM in production (Critical). (2) Stale data: no invalidation means profile updates are invisible to cached sessions (High). (3) No TTL: cache entries persist for the process lifetime (High). (4) Thread safety: Python dict is not thread-safe for concurrent writes under multi-threaded servers (Medium). Recommend using an established caching library (cachetools, Redis) with TTL, LRU eviction, and invalidation hooks.

questions.explanation: The review identifies four distinct issues with severity levels: OOM risk (unbounded growth), staleness (no invalidation), persistence (no TTL), and concurrency (thread safety). Recommending an established library addresses all issues systematically rather than patching them individually.

questions.question:
A development team of 8 is experiencing these symptoms:
- PRs average 5 days from open to merge
- 30% of builds fail on the main branch
- Developers report spending 40% of their time on code reviews
- Deployment frequency has dropped from daily to weekly

Step 1: Identify the root causes (not just symptoms).
Step 2: Determine which interventions have the highest leverage.
Step 3: Select the correct improvement plan.

Which plan addresses the root causes?

choices.A: Skip code review entirely to speed up merges
choices.B: Hire more developers to increase capacity
choices.C: Reduce the team to 4 developers to simplify coordination
choices.D: Root causes: PRs are too large (causing long reviews and frequent merge conflicts), insufficient pre-merge validation (causing main branch failures), and review bottlenecks (causing the 5-day cycle). Interventions: (1) Enforce PR size limits (<400 lines) to reduce review time and conflict probability. (2) Require CI pass before review (pre-merge validation eliminates 30% main branch failures). (3) Implement automated style/lint checks to reduce review scope. (4) Set 4-hour first-response SLA with review rotation. Expected outcome: PR cycle drops to 1-2 days, main branch stays green, deployment returns to daily.

questions.explanation: The symptoms form a reinforcing cycle: large PRs take long to review, stale PRs cause merge conflicts, conflicts cause build failures, failures cause deployment hesitancy. Breaking the cycle requires smaller PRs (faster reviews, fewer conflicts), pre-merge CI (green main branch), and review SLAs (faster cycles).

questions.question:
You need to design a secrets rotation strategy for a production system that uses:
- Database credentials (PostgreSQL)
- API keys for 3 third-party services
- TLS certificates for inter-service mTLS
- JWT signing keys for user authentication

Step 1: Classify each secret by rotation complexity and risk.
Step 2: Design the rotation mechanism for each category.
Step 3: Select the correct strategy.

Which strategy is correct?

choices.A: Rotate all secrets daily using a cron job
choices.B: Rotate all secrets on the same schedule for simplicity
choices.C: Never rotate secrets because rotation causes outages
choices.D: Database credentials: Rotate every 90 days via Vault dynamic secrets (short-lived, auto-generated). Third-party API keys: Rotate every 180 days with dual-key overlap period (old key valid for 24h after new key activation). TLS certificates: Auto-renew via cert-manager with 30-day validity and 7-day renewal window. JWT signing keys: Rotate every 30 days with JWKS endpoint listing both old and new keys, allowing gradual token expiration. Each type has different risk profiles and requires different overlap strategies.

questions.explanation: Each secret type has different rotation complexity: DB credentials can use dynamic short-lived secrets (Vault), API keys need overlap periods for third-party propagation, TLS uses automated cert management, and JWT keys need JWKS overlap for in-flight tokens. One-size-fits-all rotation ignores these differences.

questions.question:
Your team operates 12 microservices on Kubernetes. Observability is minimal: basic health checks and stdout logs. A production incident takes 4 hours to diagnose because there is no way to trace a request across services.

Step 1: Identify the three pillars of observability needed.
Step 2: Design the implementation approach.
Step 3: Select the correct observability stack.

Which approach provides the most diagnostic value?

choices.A: Add request ID headers and search logs manually
choices.B: Install an APM tool on the 3 most critical services only
choices.C: Implement all three observability pillars: (1) Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry + Jaeger/Tempo) with trace context propagation across all 12 services to follow requests end-to-end. (2) Structured logging (JSON format) with trace ID correlation, shipped to a centralized log aggregator (Loki/ELK). (3) Metrics (Prometheus + Grafana) for latency histograms, error rates, and saturation per service. Use OpenTelemetry as the unified instrumentation layer to minimize per-service integration effort.
choices.D: Add more log statements to each service

questions.explanation: The three observability pillars (traces, logs, metrics) provide complementary views: traces show request flow across services, logs provide detail at each service, metrics show system-wide health. OpenTelemetry unifies instrumentation. Trace ID correlation links all three for any specific request.

questions.question:
A developer proposes using Go for a new CRUD API service. The existing system is 100% Python. The team of 6 has no Go experience. The service has no special performance requirements.

Step 1: Evaluate the technical merit of the proposal.
Step 2: Assess the organizational impact.
Step 3: Select the correct decision.

What is the right call?

choices.A: Let the developer use Go but isolate them from the rest of the team
choices.B: Approve Go to diversify the technology stack
choices.C: Approve Go because it will make the service faster
choices.D: Decline Go for this service. The proposal lacks technical justification (no performance requirement Go would uniquely serve). Introducing a new language requires: training investment for 6 developers, separate CI/CD tooling, split operational expertise, and increased on-call complexity. The organizational cost exceeds the benefit. Revisit Go when a genuine performance or concurrency need arises that Python cannot serve.

questions.explanation: Technology diversification must be justified by genuine technical need. A CRUD API with no performance requirements does not need Go. The hidden costs (training, tooling, operational split) for a 6-person team are significant. A good coordinator prevents resume-driven development while keeping the door open for justified future use.

questions.question:
You are designing a feature flag system for a team doing trunk-based development. Requirements:
- Support boolean, percentage-based, and user-segment flags
- Flags must be evaluable in <1ms
- Flag changes must propagate to all instances within 30 seconds
- Audit log of all flag changes
- Stale flags (unused for 30+ days) should be automatically identified

Step 1: Choose between build-your-own and third-party.
Step 2: Design the propagation mechanism.
Step 3: Select the correct architecture.

Which architecture meets all requirements?

choices.A: Store flags in a database and query on every request
choices.B: Use environment variables for all flags
choices.C: Hard-code flags with if/else statements and deploy to change them
choices.D: Use a feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or custom) with: local in-memory SDK cache for <1ms evaluation, SSE/WebSocket streaming from the flag service for <30s propagation, flag change events written to an audit log (append-only), and a background job that queries SDK analytics to identify flags with zero evaluations in 30+ days for cleanup. The SDK evaluates flags locally against cached rules without network calls per evaluation.

questions.explanation: The <1ms latency requirement demands local evaluation (no network per-call). Streaming propagation meets the 30s requirement. Audit logging is a server-side concern. SDK analytics tracking evaluation counts enables automated stale flag detection. This is the standard architecture used by LaunchDarkly, Unleash, and similar systems.

questions.question:
A production system is experiencing intermittent 500 errors. Logs show the errors correlate with high traffic periods. The system architecture:
- Load balancer -> 3 API server instances -> PostgreSQL (single primary, 2 read replicas)
- Connection pool: 20 connections per instance (60 total)
- PostgreSQL max_connections: 100
- Peak traffic: 3,000 requests/second
- Average query time: 5ms, but some complex reports take 2 seconds

Step 1: Calculate whether connection exhaustion is the root cause.
Step 2: Identify all contributing factors.
Step 3: Select the correct fix.

Which analysis and fix is correct?

choices.A: Increase PostgreSQL max_connections to 1000
choices.B: The issue is the load balancer; replace it with a DNS round-robin
choices.C: Add more API server instances to distribute the load
choices.D: At 3,000 req/s with 5ms average query time, each connection handles ~200 req/s, so 60 connections handle ~12,000 req/s (sufficient). However, 2-second report queries occupy connections 400x longer than normal queries. A few concurrent reports exhaust the pool. Fix: Separate connection pools for transactional (fast) and analytical (slow) queries. Route reports to read replicas. Add connection pool monitoring. Set query timeout for the transactional pool at 500ms. This prevents slow queries from starving fast ones.

questions.explanation: The math shows normal queries have ample pool capacity. The 2-second report queries are the problem: they hold connections 400x longer, causing pool exhaustion during peaks. Separate pools isolate the blast radius. Routing reports to read replicas reduces primary load. This is the classic mixed-workload connection pool starvation pattern.

questions.question:
Your organization wants to implement a developer platform (Internal Developer Platform / IDP). The current state:
- 25 microservices, 4 teams, 3 languages (Python, Go, TypeScript)
- Each team has its own CI/CD pipeline configuration
- Onboarding a new service takes 2 weeks
- No standardized observability or deployment patterns

Step 1: Identify the highest-value IDP capabilities to build first.
Step 2: Determine the build-vs-buy decision.
Step 3: Select the correct IDP strategy.

Which strategy provides the most leverage?

choices.A: Buy a commercial PaaS like Heroku and mandate all teams use it
choices.B: Build a custom platform from scratch with all features
choices.C: Phase 1 (Golden paths): Create standardized service templates per language (Python, Go, TypeScript) with built-in CI/CD, observability, and deployment configs. Use Backstage as the service catalog and scaffolding tool. Phase 2 (Self-service): Environment provisioning via Terraform modules triggered by PR-based workflow. Phase 3 (Guardrails): Automated compliance checks, security scanning, and cost governance integrated into the golden paths. Target: reduce new service onboarding from 2 weeks to 2 hours.
choices.D: Standardize on a single language to simplify the platform

questions.explanation: The phased approach builds the highest-leverage capability first (golden paths for service templates), then adds self-service, then governance. Backstage provides the catalog/scaffolding layer. This reduces onboarding from 2 weeks to hours while preserving polyglot flexibility. A full custom build is too expensive; mandating one language is too disruptive.

questions.question:
You must coordinate a database migration from MySQL to PostgreSQL for a live production system serving 5,000 transactions per minute. The database has 50 tables, 200GB of data, and the application cannot have more than 5 minutes of downtime.

Step 1: Design the migration approach that meets the downtime constraint.
Step 2: Identify the validation strategy.
Step 3: Select the correct migration plan.

Which plan meets the 5-minute downtime constraint?

choices.A: Migrate one table at a time over several months
choices.B: Use an ORM abstraction layer so the database does not matter
choices.C: Phase 1 (Weeks before): Set up continuous replication from MySQL to PostgreSQL using a CDC tool (Debezium/pgloader). Validate data parity with checksums on both databases. Phase 2 (Pre-cutover): Run the application in dual-write mode, verifying PostgreSQL handles production queries correctly. Phase 3 (Cutover, <5 min): Stop writes, verify final replication sync, switch application connection strings to PostgreSQL, resume writes. Rollback plan: keep MySQL replication running in reverse for 48 hours post-cutover.
choices.D: Stop the application, dump MySQL, import to PostgreSQL, start the application

questions.explanation: CDC-based continuous replication keeps PostgreSQL synchronized before cutover, minimizing the actual switch time to just final sync + connection string change (<5 min). Dual-write validation proves PostgreSQL handles real queries. Reverse replication provides a rollback path. This is the standard approach for live database migrations.

questions.question:
You are coordinating an incident response for a production outage. Timeline:
- T+0: Monitoring alerts fire (5xx error rate >10%)
- T+5 min: On-call engineer identifies the failing service
- T+15 min: Root cause identified (bad database migration in latest deploy)
- T+20 min: Engineer proposes two options: (A) rollback the deployment, or (B) apply a forward-fix hotfix

The bad migration added a NOT NULL column without a default value to a table with 10 million rows. The deployment also includes 3 other critical bug fixes that customers are waiting for.

Step 1: Evaluate the rollback vs forward-fix tradeoffs.
Step 2: Assess the time-to-resolution for each option.
Step 3: Select the correct incident response.

Which response is correct?

choices.A: Rollback the deployment immediately. Reason: Rolling back is deterministic and fast (~2 min for deployment revert + migration down). Forward-fixing requires writing, testing, and deploying new code under pressure, which introduces additional risk. The 3 bug fixes can be re-deployed after the migration is fixed properly. After rollback, fix the migration (add default value or make nullable), test it, and redeploy the full release. Post-incident: add migration safety checks to CI.
choices.B: Leave the system broken while investigating more options
choices.C: Forward-fix by adding a default value to the column in production
choices.D: Always rollback; never forward-fix during incidents

questions.explanation: During an active outage, the primary goal is restoring service. Rollback is deterministic and tested (migration down scripts exist). Forward-fixing under pressure risks compounding the incident. The bug fixes can wait hours; the outage cannot. The post-incident action (migration safety checks) prevents recurrence.

questions.question:
You discover that your CI pipeline has become a security liability:
- Build artifacts are stored on a shared NFS mount accessible to all developers
- The CI service account has admin privileges on the production Kubernetes cluster
- Pipeline secrets are stored in plaintext in the CI configuration repository
- Third-party CI plugins have not been audited or version-pinned
- Build logs containing environment variables are publicly accessible within the org

Step 1: Rank these vulnerabilities by exploitability and blast radius.
Step 2: Design the remediation sequence.
Step 3: Select the correct plan.

Which plan addresses the vulnerabilities in the correct priority order?

choices.A: Only fix the plaintext secrets; the others are acceptable risks
choices.B: Priority 1 (Critical, immediate): Move secrets to a vault and rotate all exposed credentials (plaintext secrets in repo = already compromised assumption). Restrict CI service account to namespace-scoped RBAC (admin on prod K8s = cluster takeover risk). Priority 2 (High, this week): Restrict build log visibility and scrub environment variables from output. Migrate artifacts to an authenticated registry with RBAC. Priority 3 (Medium, this sprint): Audit and pin all third-party CI plugins, add plugin allowlisting.
choices.C: Fix them alphabetically by description
choices.D: Replace the entire CI system with a new provider

questions.explanation: Plaintext secrets in a repo means any repo reader has production credentials (treat as compromised, rotate immediately). CI admin on prod K8s means a CI compromise = full cluster control. These are the highest blast radius. Log exposure and artifact access are high but narrower. Plugin auditing is important but lower urgency.

questions.question:
You are coordinating the development of a new payment processing system. The team consists of:
- Dev A: Senior Python/Django, 8 years experience, strong on security
- Dev B: Mid-level React/TypeScript, 3 years experience, fast UI development
- Dev C: Senior Go, 6 years experience, strong on distributed systems
- Dev D: Junior full-stack, 1 year experience, eager but needs mentoring

The system requires: PCI-DSS compliant payment API, real-time transaction processing pipeline, admin dashboard, and webhook notification service. Timeline: 8 weeks.

Step 1: Decompose the system into work packages.
Step 2: Map work packages to developers based on expertise, security requirements, and mentoring opportunities.
Step 3: Select the correct plan.

Which plan maximizes quality, security, and team development?

choices.A: Assign everyone to pair program on the payment API first, then move to the next component
choices.B: Dev A: Payment API (Python/Django) â€” PCI-DSS compliance requires their security expertise. Dev C: Transaction processing pipeline (Go) â€” high-throughput, distributed systems work matches their strengths. Dev B: Admin dashboard (React) â€” fast UI velocity. Dev D: Webhook notification service with Dev A as mentor â€” isolated, well-defined scope suitable for junior developer with security oversight. Cross-cutting: Dev A reviews all security-sensitive PRs across all components. Dev C reviews Dev D's service architecture.
choices.C: Let Dev D choose their preferred component to maximize engagement
choices.D: Dev A handles the entire payment API alone since security is critical

questions.explanation: This plan routes security-critical work to the security expert (Dev A), distributed systems to the systems expert (Dev C), UI to the fast frontend developer (Dev B), and gives the junior developer an isolated, well-scoped component with senior mentorship. Cross-cutting security review ensures all components meet PCI-DSS. Dev D grows without risking critical path.
